title: Irreversibility Mapping

content.statement: Some decisions can be undone cheaply; others permanently narrow the future.
content.question: For your next big business decision, is it a one-way door or a two-way door? Are you treating it accordingly?
content.equation: IM = (Reversal Cost + Option Loss + Time to Undo + Damage Risk) ÷ Decision Value
sop: Before your next major decision, ask: if this goes wrong, can I undo it, and what would that cost?
Sort your pending decisions into one-way doors (hard or costly to reverse) and two-way doors (cheap to reverse).
Move fast on two-way doors, don't let them sit on your desk waiting for more analysis.
Rate Reversal Cost, Option Loss, Time to Undo, and Damage Risk 1-10, then rate Decision Value 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
For true one-way doors, add one extra step, a trusted second opinion or a 48-hour cooling period, before committing.
content.explainer: Speed on reversible decisions, slowness on irreversible ones. Founders usually get this exactly backwards.
questions: For your next big business decision, is it a one-way door or a two-way door? Are you treating it accordingly?
Which decision are you agonizing over that you could actually reverse cheaply if it went wrong?
Which decision are you about to make fast that you can't actually take back?
content.equation_example: Rate Reversal Cost, Option Loss, Time to Undo, and Damage Risk 1-10 each, then rate Decision Value 1-10. Reversal Cost 7, Option Loss 6, Time to Undo 5, Damage Risk 8, sum 26, Decision Value 8 → IM = 26 ÷ 8 = 3.25. Higher IM means the decision is genuinely hard to walk back, so it deserves more analysis time; lower means it's cheap to reverse, so speed matters more than certainty.
content.closing_statement: Move fast on two-way doors, slow on one-way.

caption:
Principle Nº 35: Irreversibility Mapping

Some decisions can be undone cheaply; others permanently narrow the future.

Ask yourself: For your next big business decision, is it a one-way door or a two-way door? Are you treating it accordingly?
